theme.bgGradient: radial-gradient(1200px 700px at 12% -10%, rgba(56,189,248,.16), transparent 60%), radial-gradient(1000px 600px at 100% 0%, rgba(129,140,248,.16), transparent 55%), linear-gradient(160deg, #0A0F24, #111A3A 55%, #16224D)

title: DSLs as Harnesses
description: Why a deliberately limited language is one of the most reliable harnesses for an AI agent — the four harness properties a DSL delivers for free, three concrete examples (MulmoScript, Collections, vibe crafting), the escape-hatch hazard, and why this matters more as models improve.

beats.text: D S Ls as harnesses. Why a deliberately limited language is one of the most reliable ways to give an AI agent a place to stand.

slide.title: DSLs as **Harnesses**
slide.subtitle: Why a deliberately limited language gives an agent {primary:a place to stand}

beats.text: The prevailing lesson of applied AI engineering is that the harness — the designed environment an agent operates inside — matters more than the model. For a language model, the interface is not a convenience layer. The interface is the cognitive architecture.

slide.quote: The interface is not a convenience layer wrapped around the intelligence. For a language model, {primary:**the interface is the cognitive architecture.**}
slide.author: And a DSL is a powerful harness *precisely because it gives up power.*

beats.text: Why does the trade pay off? A general-purpose language is Turing complete — that is its glory and, for an agent, its hazard. Infinite ways to write anything, no conventions, no signal of domain correctness. A DSL inverts every one of those properties.

items.text: Turing-complete — infinite ways to express anything
Freedom under uncertainty → agents **thrash and drift**

right.title: Domain-Specific Language

items.text: **Reshapes the search space** the agent explores

right.footer: Well-formed or rejected — before anything executes

callout.text: Handing an agent a DSL isn't giving it a tool — it's {primary:**reshaping the space it has to search.**}
callout.label: Key move

beats.text: From that single design decision, the four classic harness patterns fall out for free. The schema is the system of record. The validator is a free feedback loop. The grammar is a forcing function. And the unit of the language is the unit of context.

slide.title: What a DSL gives you for free

items.title: 1 · The schema is the system of record.
items.description: Intent lives in a structured, inspectable artifact — diffable, versionable, checkable without running it.

items.title: 2 · The validator is a free feedback loop.
items.description: Malformed output is rejected at the moment of introduction, with a localized, actionable message.

items.title: 3 · The grammar is a forcing function.
items.description: A finite vocabulary structurally shrinks the room to hallucinate — the agent can only compose real primitives.

items.title: 4 · The unit of the language is the unit of context.
items.description: A beat, a rule, a record — the agent edits one piece without holding the whole document in mind.

beats.text: First example: MulmoScript, a JSON DSL for multimodal presentations. The key architecture: the agent does not produce the video — it produces the script. A deterministic renderer turns that script into the finished artifact.

slide.title: Generation and execution, cleanly separated
slide.subtitle: The agent writes the *script* — never the pixels

content.value: Inspectable before a single frame renders:
The deterministic work — code that never guesses:

callout.text: The model decides *what* to think about; the harness decides *how* it is executed — in MulmoScript, an actual boundary in the pipeline.
callout.label: Boundary

beats.text: Second example: Collections — a DSL that enforces its own invariants, and whose authoring pen is handed to the end user. A schema declares fields, relationships, recurrence and notifications; the loader rejects an incoherent schema before it is ever used.

content.value: By declaring a schema, a **non-engineer designs the environment the agent works in.**

items: What counts as "done", when to notify

items.text: Money field with no currency source
`ref` to a collection that doesn't exist
`spawn` born already matching its predicate — an unbounded respawn

content.value: Each failure: a precise, actionable message — no human in the loop.

beats.text: This is what vibe crafting looks like from the user's chair. Where vibe coding lets a developer conjure software, vibe crafting lets an end user conjure a working data app. But possible is not the same as safe — and what closes that gap is not a better model. It is the harness.

items: Describes intent, model fills in code
Owns the failure when it breaks
"Add a priority field." "Make rent recur monthly."
Never sees a schema or a line of HTML
Cannot audit output — **and shouldn't have to**

callout.text: The model supplies the expressiveness; the DSL, validators, and sandbox supply {highlight:**the safety the user can no longer supply for themselves.**} Take the harness away and you have an untrained user holding a loaded code generator.
callout.label: The gap

beats.text: One hazard: the same rigidity that makes a DSL a good harness is also its failure mode. A DSL that cannot express what the user needs becomes a cage. Well-designed DSLs all answer this the same way — with a deliberate exit to a more expressive layer.

slide.title: Design the **escape hatch**
slide.subtitle: When the language runs out, provide a clean, legible path to a more expressive layer

items.title: Collections → seeded chat
items.description: When a record needs human judgment, an action button opens an agent chat with full tools

items.title: Schema → custom HTML view
items.description: A portfolio as a chart, restaurants as a map — sandboxed arbitrary code instead of contorted field types

items.title: MulmoScript → external assets
items.description: Arbitrary media and raw HTML, rather than forcing everything into its own primitives

slide.footer: A harness with no escape hatch eventually forces the agent to fail or lie — both worse than a slightly leakier abstraction.

beats.text: It's tempting to think DSLs are a crutch for weak models. The opposite is true. The case for a DSL is strongest exactly where we delegate autonomously and over long horizons — and those conditions matter more as we trust agents with more.

eyebrow.label: Why this matters more as models improve

content.value: "Smarter models can handle general-purpose code — {danger:drop the guardrails.}"
But the conditions of serious agentic work are exactly where DSLs shine:

items.text: The **model** is the reasoning engine — what to say, build, express
The **DSL** decides what it gets to reason about — and guarantees the rest
A smarter model in a harness = capability aimed at the part that needs *judgment*

beats.text: Getting that boundary right is the whole game — and a well-chosen DSL draws it with unusual precision.

slide.quote: Getting that boundary right is the whole game — and a well-chosen DSL draws it with {primary:**unusual precision.**}
slide.author: *DSLs as Harnesses* · 2026
